title: Employment (Allocation of Tips) Act 2023
chapters: 15 sections (inserting new ss.27D–27Q into the Employment Rights Act 1996)

parliamentaryOrigin.bill: Employment (Allocation of Tips) Bill [Private Members' Bill]
parliamentaryOrigin.introducedBy: Dean Russell MP (Conservative, Watford) — Private Members' Bill via ballot procedure
commonsSponsors: Virginia Crosbie MP (successor sponsor, Ynys Môn — took over when Russell became Minister September 2022)
parliamentaryOrigin.commencementNotes: Most provisions commenced 1 October 2024 by SI 2024/829 (Commencement No. 2 Regulations). s.9 (power to issue Code of Practice) commenced on Royal Assent (2 May 2023). Code of Practice approved by HC 15 May 2024 and HL 24 May 2024, in force 1 October 2024. Non-statutory guidance published 27 September 2024.

secondReadingCommons.summary: Dean Russell MP moved the Second Reading, framing the Bill around fairness: 'People who work in hospitality should not need to rely on tips as part of their salary... if we give somebody some money to say thank you, they should be able to keep that money.' Passed without division with cross-party support. Unite the union's long-running fair tips campaign acknowledged by multiple members.

codeOfPracticeDebate.summary: Kevin Hollinrake (Minister) presented the statutory Code of Practice on Fair and Transparent Distribution of Tips. Opposition spokesperson noted it had been seven years since action was first promised and estimated workers had missed out on £200 million per year in lost tips — over £1 billion total. Virginia Crosbie (sponsor) emphasised that 18% of workers in Ynys Môn are in tourism/hospitality, estimating £900,000 additional income for workers in her constituency alone.

significantCommitteeAmendments.amendment: No amendments tabled at Committee stage
significantCommitteeAmendments.summary: The Bill passed through Public Bill Committee on 12 October 2022 without any amendments being tabled — indicating the high level of cross-party consensus. Committee was chaired by Siobhain McDonagh MP.

precedingDocuments.title: Government consultation on tips, gratuities, cover and service charges (2016)
precedingDocuments.significance: The 2016 consultation followed media exposés of hospitality chains retaining staff tips. 88% of respondents supported legislative reform. Government response (October 2018) confirmed intention to legislate.

precedingDocuments.title: Government response: Tips, Gratuities, Cover and Service Charges (October 2018)
precedingDocuments.significance: Government committed to legislating to ensure tips are passed to workers in full. Delayed by COVID-19 pandemic and parliamentary scheduling constraints.

precedingDocuments.title: Unite the union 'Fair Tips' campaign
precedingDocuments.significance: Long-running Unite campaign that built public and political momentum for legislative reform. Multiple media investigations (2015-2022) into chains retaining or deducting from staff tips — including Pizza Express, Giraffe, Wahaca, and others.

precedingDocuments.title: COVID-19 pandemic impact on tipping practices
precedingDocuments.significance: The pandemic triggered a societal shift from cash to card payments, significantly reducing cash tips. This increased the proportion of tips processed through employer payment systems, making the issue of employer retention more acute and increasing political urgency for reform.

parliamentaryOrigin.changeOfSponsor: Dean Russell MP introduced the Bill on 15 June 2022. On 20 September 2022, Russell was appointed Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State at BEIS, making him the responsible Minister for his own Bill. Virginia Crosbie MP (Ynys Môn) took over as sponsor. Russell served as Minister for the Bill from 20 September to 27 October 2022 during his period in government.

leadingCases.name: No reported tribunal decisions under the Tips Act 2023 as at March 2026
leadingCases.citation: N/A — Act in force from 1 October 2024
leadingCases.principle: The Act has been in force for approximately six months as at March 2026. Given the 12-month limitation period for complaints under s.27K (inserted as new ERA 1996 s.27K), the first tribunal decisions under the Act are anticipated from late 2025 onwards.
leadingCases.significance: SURVEILLANCE — Eileen must monitor BAILII and ET Register for the first decisions interpreting 'fair allocation', the scope of 'qualifying tips', and the interaction between the Code of Practice and tribunal determinations.

leadingCases.name: Saha v Viewpoint Field Services Ltd (ET, 2017)
leadingCases.principle: Pre-Act case establishing that service charges retained by employer and not paid to staff constituted unauthorised deductions from wages under ERA 1996 s.13 — but only where the contractual position supported the claim that tips formed part of wages. This route was uncertain and case-specific; the 2023 Act provides the standalone statutory right.
leadingCases.heldText: Where a service charge is contractually designated as payable to staff, employer retention constitutes an unauthorised deduction from wages.
leadingCases.significance: Demonstrates why the 2023 Act was needed — the pre-existing s.13 ERA 1996 deductions route was uncertain and required the tips to be contractually part of wages. The new s.27D provides a standalone obligation regardless of contractual position.

parts.title: Part 1 — Core Obligation: Fair Allocation of Tips

sections.title: Tips, gratuities and service charges — fair allocation obligation
sections.summary: Creates the central statutory obligation: an employer must ensure that the total amount of qualifying tips, gratuities and service charges paid at, or otherwise attributable to, a place of business is allocated fairly between workers at that place of business. Where tips are 'employer-received' (processed through the employer's payment system), the allocated amount is payable by the employer to the worker. Fairness must be assessed with regard to the statutory Code of Practice.
sections.text:
After section 27C of the Employment Rights Act 1996 insert—

27D Tips, gratuities and service charges

(1) An employer must ensure that the total amount of the qualifying tips, gratuities and service charges paid at, or otherwise attributable to, a place of business of the employer is allocated fairly between workers of the employer at that place of business.

(2) Where a worker is allocated an amount of employer-received tips in accordance with subsection (1), that amount is payable to the worker by the employer.

(3) In determining what would be a fair allocation of qualifying tips, gratuities and service charges under this section or section 27E (non-public places of business), regard must be had to the relevant provisions of any code of practice issued under this Part.

(4) See also sections 27E (non-public places of business) and 27F (independent troncs).
sections.keyPrinciple: 100% of qualifying tips must be allocated to workers. The employer cannot retain any portion. 'Fair' allocation must take account of the Code of Practice — the Code is not optional guidance, it has statutory weight in tribunal proceedings.
refs: Code of Practice on Fair and Transparent Distribution of Tips (in force 1 October 2024)
ERA 1996 s.27E (non-public places of business — e.g. delivery drivers, call centres)
ERA 1996 s.27F (independent troncs — tronc operated by independent tronc master)
ERA 1996 s.27G (time limit for dealing with tips — end of following month)
ERA 1996 s.13 (protection of wages — pre-existing right, now supplemented by s.27D)

regulatoryGuidance.title: Code of Practice on Fair and Transparent Distribution of Tips
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Statutory code of practice issued under s.27P ERA 1996. Covers scope of qualifying tips, fairness in allocation, methods of distribution, transparency, and problem resolution. Tribunals must have regard to the Code.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Non-statutory guidance on the Employment (Allocation of Tips) Act 2023
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Published 27 September 2024. Provides additional examples, basic templates for tipping policies and records requests. Confirms agency workers must be considered in tip distribution.

commonErrors: Retaining any percentage of employer-received tips — whether as 'admin charges', 'processing fees', or 'breakage charges' — the Act requires 100% allocation to workers
Failing to have regard to the Code of Practice when determining fairness — the Code has statutory weight and tribunals must consider it
Excluding back-of-house workers (kitchen staff, porters, cleaners) from tip allocation without justification — the Code makes clear that all workers who contribute to the service may be included
Treating 'qualifying tips' narrowly — the Act covers tips, gratuities AND service charges paid at or attributable to the place of business, whether paid by card, cash, or any other method
Confusing 'fairness' with 'equality' — fair allocation does not require equal shares. The Code permits differentiation based on role, seniority, hours worked, and customer interaction

Using tips to supplement sub-NMW pay — NMW Regulations 2015 Reg.10(j) expressly excludes tips from NMW calculation. Tips are ALWAYS additional to wages, never a substitute. See Part 7 cross-reference.

sections.title: Non-public places of business — delivery, online, and remote services
sections.summary: Extends the fair allocation obligation to non-public places of business — premises where customers are not normally present but from which services are provided. This covers delivery drivers, call centre workers, online order fulfilment staff, and any worker whose employer receives tips attributable to their work even though the customer is not physically present. The employer must take all reasonable steps to ensure that qualifying tips attributable to a non-public place of business are allocated fairly.
sections.text:
After section 27D of the Employment Rights Act 1996 insert—

27E Non-public places of business

(1) This section applies where a place of business of an employer is not one at which customers of the employer are normally present when they pay qualifying tips, gratuities and service charges.

(2) The employer must take all reasonable steps to ensure that the total amount of the qualifying tips, gratuities and service charges that are attributable to the place of business is allocated fairly between workers of the employer at the place of business.

(3) In determining what would be a fair allocation for the purposes of this section, regard must be had to the relevant provisions of any code of practice issued under this Part.
sections.keyPrinciple: The Act does not only apply to restaurants, bars, and hotels. Any employer whose workers generate tips through remote, delivery, or online services is covered. The obligation is to take 'all reasonable steps' — a slightly lower standard than the absolute obligation at public-facing premises, reflecting the practical difficulty of tracking tips attributable to non-public locations.
refs: ERA 1996 s.27D (fair allocation at public places — the primary obligation)
ERA 1996 s.27Q (definition of 'place of business' — any premises from which the employer carries on business)
Code of Practice — scope section covering delivery and online services
commonErrors: Assuming the Act only applies to restaurants and hotels — it covers ALL employers who receive qualifying tips, including food delivery companies, taxi/ride-hailing services, and online service providers

Excluding delivery drivers or kitchen staff at a takeaway-only premises from tip allocation on the basis that no customers are physically present — s.27E was specifically designed to prevent this exclusion
Failing to track tips attributable to online/delivery orders separately from dine-in tips — the allocation obligation requires consideration of all qualifying tips by place of business

sections.title: Independent troncs
sections.summary: Where an employer makes arrangements for an independent tronc operator to allocate qualifying tips, and it is fair for the employer to make those arrangements, the employer is treated as having complied with the s.27D(1) fair allocation obligation. The tronc operator must be genuinely independent of the employer. This preserves the well-established tronc system used in many hospitality businesses while subjecting it to the fairness standard.
sections.text:
After section 27E of the Employment Rights Act 1996 insert—

27F Independent troncs

(1) In this section 'relevant tips' means the qualifying tips, gratuities and service charges that—

(a) are paid at, or are otherwise attributable to, a place of business of an employer, and

(b) are employer-received tips.

(2) Where—

(a) the employer makes arrangements for the total amount of the relevant tips to be allocated between workers of the employer at the place of business by an independent tronc operator, and

(b) it is fair for the employer to make those arrangements,

the employer is to be treated as having ensured that the total amount of the relevant tips is allocated fairly between workers of the employer at the place of business in accordance with section 27D(1).
sections.keyPrinciple: A genuinely independent tronc provides a safe harbour for employers — if the tronc operator is independent and the arrangements are fair, the employer is deemed to have complied. But 'independence' is a real requirement: the employer cannot control how the tronc master allocates tips.
refs: ERA 1996 s.27D (fair allocation obligation)
HMRC guidance on tronc arrangements and tax treatment
Code of Practice on Fair and Transparent Distribution of Tips (tronc section)
commonErrors: Appointing a 'tronc master' who is the employer's general manager or a person who takes direction from the employer on allocation — the tronc operator must be genuinely independent
Assuming that using a tronc automatically satisfies the fairness obligation — the arrangements must themselves be fair
Failing to document the independence of the tronc operator — in any tribunal complaint, the employer would need to demonstrate genuine independence

sections.title: When tips etc must be dealt with — end of the following month
sections.summary: Qualifying tips must be dealt with no later than the end of the month following the month in which they were paid by the customer. This creates a hard deadline: a tip paid in January must be allocated and paid to the worker by the end of February at the latest.
sections.text:
After section 27F of the Employment Rights Act 1996 insert—

27G When tips etc must be dealt with

(1) An employer must ensure that the employer-received qualifying tips, gratuities and service charges that are paid at, or otherwise attributable to, a place of business of the employer are allocated and paid in accordance with section 27D no later than the end of the month following the month in which they were paid by customers or other third parties.

(2) In the case of a non-employer-received qualifying tip, gratuity or service charge, the employer must take all reasonable steps to ensure that the tip is dealt with in accordance with section 27D or 27E no later than the end of the month following the month in which the tip was paid by the customer or other third party.
sections.keyPrinciple: The 'end of following month' deadline is absolute for employer-received tips. For non-employer-received tips (e.g. cash tips given directly to workers), the employer must take 'all reasonable steps' — a lower standard reflecting the practical difficulty of controlling cash tips.
refs: ERA 1996 s.27D (fair allocation)
ERA 1996 s.27K (tribunal complaints for failure to comply with s.27G)
ERA 1996 s.23 (complaints about unauthorised deductions — parallel but distinct route)
commonErrors: Paying tips quarterly or monthly in arrears beyond the statutory deadline — the Act requires payment by the end of the month following the month the tip was paid by the customer
Treating the deadline as guidance rather than a hard statutory obligation — failure to meet it is a standalone breach enforceable at tribunal

parts.title: Part 2 — Transparency Obligations: Policy and Records

sections.title: Written policy on tips
sections.summary: Employers who receive qualifying tips 'more than on an occasional and exceptional basis' must have a written policy on the allocation of tips. The policy must be made available to all workers of the employer at the place of business. This is a mandatory transparency obligation — not having a written policy is a standalone breach.
sections.text:
After section 27H of the Employment Rights Act 1996 insert—

27I Written policy on tips

(1) This section applies where qualifying tips, gratuities and service charges are paid at, or are otherwise attributable to, a place of business of an employer more than on an occasional and exceptional basis.

(2) The employer must have a written policy on how the employer ensures (or intends to ensure) that qualifying tips, gratuities and service charges paid at, or otherwise attributable to, the place of business are dealt with in accordance with sections 27D, 27E and 27G.

(3) The employer must make the policy available to all workers of the employer at the place of business.
sections.keyPrinciple: If your business regularly receives tips, you must have a written tipping policy and make it available to all workers. The threshold is deliberately low — 'more than on an occasional and exceptional basis' captures any business where tips are a normal feature.
refs: Code of Practice on Fair and Transparent Distribution of Tips (template policy)
Non-statutory guidance (September 2024 — basic template for tipping policies)
ERA 1996 s.27N (tribunal complaints for breach of policy/records obligations)

regulatoryGuidance.title: Non-statutory guidance — tipping policy template
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Contains basic template for written tipping policy that employers can adapt for their business

commonErrors: Not having a written tipping policy at all — this is a standalone breach even if tips are actually being allocated fairly
Having a policy but not making it available to all workers — the obligation is to make it available to ALL workers at the place of business, not just front-of-house staff
Setting the threshold too high — 'more than on an occasional and exceptional basis' is a very low bar. A café that regularly receives card tips meets this threshold

sections.title: Records of tips — three-year retention obligation
sections.summary: Employers must keep a record of qualifying tips, gratuities and service charges received at each place of business and how they were allocated and distributed. Records must be kept for a minimum of three years. Workers have a right to request access to the records relating to their own tips.
sections.text:
After section 27I of the Employment Rights Act 1996 insert—

27J Records

(1) An employer must keep a record, in respect of each place of business of the employer, of qualifying tips, gratuities and service charges paid at, or otherwise attributable to, the place of business.

(2) The record must show how the qualifying tips, gratuities and service charges were allocated and dealt with.

(3) The record must be retained for a period of at least 3 years beginning with the date on which the qualifying tip, gratuity or service charge was paid by the customer or other third party.
sections.keyPrinciple: Three-year record retention is mandatory. Records must show both the total tips received AND how they were allocated and distributed. This creates a clear audit trail that tribunals can examine.
refs: ERA 1996 s.27J(4)–(8) (worker's right to request access to tipping records)
ERA 1996 s.27N (tribunal complaints for failure to keep records)
DPA 2018 / UK GDPR (records containing personal data about workers' earnings are subject to data protection requirements)
Non-statutory guidance (September 2024 — template for tipping records request)
commonErrors: Not keeping records at all — relying on memory or informal systems that cannot be produced if a tribunal complaint is made
Keeping records for less than three years — the retention period runs from the date the tip was paid by the customer, not from the date it was distributed to workers
Refusing worker requests to access tipping records — workers have a statutory right to request access, and the employer must respond within one month

parts.title: Part 3 — Agency Workers and Non-Public Businesses

sections.title: Agency workers — tips allocation
sections.summary: Extends the fair allocation obligation to agency workers. Where agency workers work alongside directly employed workers at a place of business, the employer must take all reasonable steps to ensure that qualifying tips are also allocated fairly to agency workers. The principal employer's obligation extends to ensuring that the agency or intermediary passes on the allocated amount.
sections.text:
After section 27G of the Employment Rights Act 1996 insert—

27H Agency workers

(1) This section applies where an agency worker works at a place of business of an employer alongside workers of the employer.

(2) The employer must take all reasonable steps to ensure that qualifying tips, gratuities and service charges paid at, or otherwise attributable to, the place of business are also allocated fairly to agency workers.

(3) Where an agency worker is allocated an amount under subsection (2), the employer must take all reasonable steps to ensure that the amount is paid to the agency worker by the agent.

(4) The agent must pay the agency worker the amount referred to in subsection (3) no later than the end of the month following the month in which it was allocated.
sections.keyPrinciple: Agency workers are not excluded from tip allocation. Where they work alongside direct employees and contribute to the service, they must be included in the fair allocation. The non-statutory guidance (September 2024) confirmed this expressly.
refs: Agency Workers Regulations 2010 (SI 2010/93) — separate but related worker protections
ERA 1996 s.27D (fair allocation obligation — primary provision)
ERA 1996 s.27K(2) (agency workers' separate right to bring tribunal complaint against the agent)
Non-statutory guidance (September 2024 — confirms agency workers must be considered)
commonErrors: Excluding agency workers from tip pools entirely — the Act expressly covers agency workers alongside direct employees

Assuming the employment agency is solely responsible for paying agency workers their tip allocation — the principal employer bears the obligation to ensure fair allocation, and the agent bears the obligation to make the payment

parts.title: Part 4 — Enforcement: Tribunal Complaints and Remedies

sections.title: Tribunal complaints — unfair allocation and failure to pay
sections.summary: Creates the enforcement mechanism: a worker may present a complaint to an employment tribunal that the employer has failed to comply with the fair allocation obligation (s.27D) or the time limit for payment (s.27G). The limitation period is 12 months from the date of the failure — unusually long compared to the standard 3-month limitation for most employment tribunal claims. The tribunal can order the employer to revise the allocation, make a payment, and award compensation of up to £5,000.
sections.text:
After section 27J of the Employment Rights Act 1996 insert—

27K Complaints to employment tribunals: tips etc

(1) A worker may present a complaint to an employment tribunal that the worker's employer has failed to comply with—

(a) section 27D (how tips etc must be dealt with), or

(b) section 27G (when tips etc must be dealt with).

(2) An eligible agency worker may also present a complaint to an employment tribunal that an agent has failed to comply with the requirement to make a payment to the eligible agency worker that the agent was required to make under section 27H(4).

(3) An employment tribunal must not consider a complaint under this section unless it is presented before the end of the period of twelve months beginning with the date of the failure to comply.
sections.keyPrinciple: The 12-month limitation period is a deliberate policy choice — significantly longer than the standard 3-month limit for unfair dismissal and deductions claims. This reflects the power imbalance: workers in hospitality are often in precarious employment and may not immediately bring complaints about tip allocation while still employed.
refs: ERA 1996 s.27L (remedies — tribunal can order revised allocation and compensation up to £5,000)
ERA 1996 s.27K(3) (12-month limitation period — compare with s.111 ERA 1996 3-month limit for unfair dismissal)
ERA 1996 s.207B (ACAS early conciliation time extension applies)
ERA 1996 s.27M (detriment — workers protected from detriment for bringing tipping complaints)
commonErrors: Assuming the standard 3-month limitation applies — the Tips Act has its own 12-month limitation period for complaints about allocation and payment

Subjecting workers to detriment for raising concerns about tipping practices — s.27M expressly protects workers from detriment, and s.104A ERA 1996 (as amended) protects against automatic unfair dismissal

sections.title: Remedies — revision orders, payment orders, and compensation
sections.summary: Where a tribunal upholds a complaint under s.27K (unfair allocation or late payment), it may: (a) make a declaration, (b) order the employer to revise the allocation of tips, (c) order the employer to make a payment to the worker, and (d) award compensation of such amount as the tribunal considers just and equitable, subject to a maximum of £5,000. The £5,000 cap is per complaint, not per worker — a worker with multiple separate failures could bring multiple complaints.
sections.keyPrinciple: The tribunal has four distinct remedial powers: declaration, revision order, payment order, AND compensation. The £5,000 compensation cap is per complaint. A worker who brings multiple well-founded complaints (e.g. for different months of unfair allocation) could receive multiple awards.
refs: ERA 1996 s.27K (complaint route — 12-month limitation)
ERA 1996 s.27N (separate complaint for policy/records — also has £5,000 cap)
ERA 1996 s.124 (compensatory award for unfair dismissal — compare: £115,115 cap vs £5,000 here)
ERA 1996 s.207B (ACAS early conciliation applies)
commonErrors: Assuming the £5,000 cap is the total exposure — it is per complaint, and workers can bring multiple complaints for different failures or different time periods
Overlooking that the tribunal can ORDER revised allocation — this is a prospective remedy that changes how tips are distributed going forward, not just compensating for past failures

sections.title: Tribunal complaints — written policy and records failures
sections.summary: Separate tribunal complaint route for failures to maintain a written tipping policy (s.27I) or to keep tipping records (s.27J). This has a shorter 3-month limitation period (compared to the 12-month period for allocation complaints). The tribunal can make a declaration and award compensation of up to £5,000.
sections.text:
After section 27M of the Employment Rights Act 1996 insert—

27N Complaints to employment tribunals: written policy and records

(1) A worker may present a complaint to an employment tribunal that the worker's employer has failed to comply with a requirement in section 27I (written policy) or 27J (records).

(2) An employment tribunal must not consider a complaint under this section unless it is presented before the end of the period of three months beginning with the date of the failure to comply.

(3) But, if the employment tribunal is satisfied that it was not reasonably practicable for a complaint to be presented before the end of the relevant period of three months, the tribunal may consider the complaint if it is presented within such further period as the tribunal considers reasonable.
sections.keyPrinciple: Policy and records failures carry a standard 3-month limitation — shorter than the 12-month period for allocation complaints. Compensation is capped at £5,000 per complaint. This is a separate and additional complaint route — a worker can bring both an allocation complaint AND a records complaint.
refs: ERA 1996 s.27I (written policy obligation)
ERA 1996 s.27J (records obligation — 3-year retention)
ERA 1996 s.27K (allocation complaints — 12-month limitation, separate route)
ERA 1996 s.111 (standard 3-month limitation for unfair dismissal — same standard applies here)

parts.title: Part 5 — Code of Practice and Interpretation

sections.title: Code of practice on fair and transparent distribution of tips
sections.summary: Empowers the Secretary of State to issue a statutory Code of Practice on the fair and transparent distribution of tips. The Code was published 22 April 2024, approved by both Houses (HC 15 May 2024, HL 24 May 2024), and came into force 1 October 2024 alongside the Act's main provisions. An employment tribunal must have regard to the Code when determining whether an employer has complied with the fair allocation obligation.
sections.text:
After section 27O of the Employment Rights Act 1996 insert—

27P Code of practice

(1) The Secretary of State must, before the end of the period of 12 months beginning with the day on which section 9 of the Employment (Allocation of Tips) Act 2023 comes into force, issue a code of practice on the fair and transparent distribution of qualifying tips, gratuities and service charges.

(2) An employment tribunal must have regard to the code of practice in proceedings under section 27K.

(3) A failure on the part of any person to observe a provision of the code of practice does not of itself make the person liable to any proceedings.
sections.keyPrinciple: The Code of Practice has statutory weight — tribunals MUST have regard to it. But failure to follow the Code does not of itself create liability — it is one factor the tribunal considers when assessing fairness. This is the same model used by the ACAS Code of Practice on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures.
refs: Code of Practice on Fair and Transparent Distribution of Tips (1 October 2024)
ACAS Code of Practice on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures — same 'have regard' model
ERA 1996 s.207A (adjustment of awards for failure to comply with Code — applies to ACAS Code but NOT the Tips Code)
ERA 1996 s.27K (tribunal complaints must have regard to Code)
commonErrors: Treating the Code as non-binding and ignoring it — while failure to follow the Code is not automatic liability, tribunals must have regard to it and departure from the Code will require justification

Confusing the Tips Code with the ACAS Code — the ERA 1996 s.207A uplift mechanism (25% adjustment of awards) applies to the ACAS Code but does NOT apply to the Tips Code. The Tips Code has its own standalone enforcement through s.27K.

sections.title: Interpretation — qualifying tips, employer-received tips, and scope
sections.summary: Defines the scope of the Act through key definitions. 'Qualifying tips' are tips, gratuities and service charges paid at or attributable to a place of business where the employer receives or exercises control or significant influence over them. Tips given directly to a worker in cash without any employer involvement fall OUTSIDE the scope of the Act. 'Employer-received tips' are the subset processed through the employer's payment system.
sections.text:
Key definitions inserted into ERA 1996:

'Qualifying tips, gratuities and service charges' — tips paid at or attributable to a place of business where:

(a) the employer receives them (employer-received tips — e.g. card payments), or

(b) the employer exercises control or significant influence over the allocation of the tips.

Tips given directly to a worker in cash without any employer involvement are NOT qualifying tips for the purposes of the Act.

'Employer-received tips' — tips received by the employer (including through payment card processing).

'Place of business' — any premises at or from which the employer carries on business, including mobile or temporary premises.

'Worker' — has the same meaning as in ERA 1996 s.230(3) — includes employees and workers but does not include genuinely self-employed independent contractors.
sections.keyPrinciple: The Act catches all tips processed through or controlled by the employer. Cash tips given directly to a worker without any employer involvement are outside scope — but this is a narrow exclusion. If the employer has a policy on cash tips (e.g. requiring them to be pooled), that constitutes 'control or significant influence' and brings the cash tips within scope.
refs: ERA 1996 s.230(3) (definition of 'worker' — includes employees and limb (b) workers)
ERA 1996 s.27D (fair allocation obligation — applies to qualifying tips)
Code of Practice (scope section — examples of qualifying vs non-qualifying tips)
commonErrors: Assuming cash tips are always outside scope — they are only outside scope if the employer has NO involvement or control. An employer policy requiring cash tips to be pooled brings them within scope
Applying the Act only to employees — the definition of 'worker' in ERA 1996 s.230(3) is broader than 'employee' and includes many casual and zero-hours workers in hospitality
Excluding delivery platform workers without analysis — whether a platform exercises 'control or significant influence' over tip allocation requires case-by-case assessment

parts.title: Part 6 — Worker Protection: Detriment and Automatic Unfair Dismissal

sections.num: s.6 (new ERA s.27M) + s.11 (ERA s.104A amendment)
sections.title: Worker protection — detriment and automatic unfair dismissal for tipping complaints
sections.summary: Two layers of worker protection. First, s.27M protects workers from being subjected to any detriment by any act or deliberate failure to act by the employer on the ground that the worker brought (or proposed to bring) a complaint under s.27K or s.27N, or did anything to enforce their tipping rights. Second, s.104A of the ERA 1996 (as amended by s.11 of the Tips Act) makes it automatically unfair to dismiss an employee for asserting their tipping rights — no qualifying period of service required. These provisions are critical: they prevent employers from retaliating against workers who raise concerns about tipping practices.
sections.text:
27M Detriment

(1) A worker has the right not to be subjected to any detriment by any act, or any deliberate failure to act, by the worker's employer done on the ground that the worker—

(a) brought proceedings under section 27K or 27N,

(b) alleged that the employer had infringed a right of the worker under sections 27D to 27J, or

(c) did any other thing under sections 27D to 27Q.

Employment Rights Act 1996, section 104A (as amended by Tips Act 2023 s.11):

An employee who is dismissed shall be regarded as unfairly dismissed if the reason (or, if more than one, the principal reason) for the dismissal is that the employee—

(a) brought proceedings under section 27K or 27N,

(b) alleged that the employer had infringed a right of the employee under sections 27D to 27J, or

(c) did any other thing under sections 27D to 27Q.
sections.keyPrinciple: A worker who raises concerns about unfair tip allocation, makes a SAR for tipping records, or brings a tribunal complaint is protected from ALL forms of retaliation. If dismissed for asserting tipping rights, the dismissal is AUTOMATICALLY unfair — no two-year qualifying period, no cap on the basic award. This mirrors the protection structure for whistleblowers (PIDA 1998) and health and safety representatives.
refs: ERA 1996 s.104A (automatic unfair dismissal — tipping rights assertion)
ERA 1996 s.47B (detriment for whistleblowing — similar protection structure)
ERA 1996 s.100 (automatic unfair dismissal for H&S — similar 'no qualifying period' model)
ERA 1996 s.108(3) (qualifying period exclusions — s.104A is listed as an exclusion)
ERA 1996 s.27K (tribunal complaint triggering protection)
ERA 1996 s.27N (policy/records complaint triggering protection)
commonErrors: Dismissing or disciplining a worker who raises concerns about tip allocation — this is automatically unfair dismissal with no qualifying period and potentially uncapped compensation

Reducing a worker's hours, changing their shifts, or excluding them from favourable rotas after they complained about tipping — any detrimental treatment on the ground of asserting tipping rights is unlawful
Assuming that the worker needs two years' service to claim unfair dismissal for a tipping complaint — s.104A removes the qualifying period entirely, mirroring the whistleblowing protection model

parts.title: Part 7 — Cross-Cutting: NMW Exclusion and Tax Treatment

sections.title: Interaction with National Minimum Wage and tax obligations
sections.summary: Two critical cross-cutting issues. First, tips CANNOT count towards the National Minimum Wage — this has been the position since the NMW Regulations 2009 amendment (removing tips from NMW calculation) and the Tips Act reinforces it. An employer who pays NMW and then argues that tips bring total pay to an acceptable level is in breach of NMW law. Second, the tax treatment of tips depends on how they are allocated: tips allocated by the employer through payroll are subject to PAYE income tax and Class 1 NICs (employer AND employee); tips allocated by a genuinely independent tronc operator are subject to income tax via the tronc PAYE scheme but NOT subject to employer NICs — a significant cost saving.
sections.text:
National Minimum Wage:

The National Minimum Wage Regulations 2015 (SI 2015/621), Regulation 10(j), expressly excludes tips, gratuities, service charges, and cover charges from the calculation of National Minimum Wage pay. This means:

(1) An employer must pay at least the NMW/NLW rate BEFORE any tip income is considered.

(2) Tips are additional to wages — they cannot be used to 'top up' sub-NMW pay.

(3) This prohibition applies regardless of whether tips are employer-received or worker-received.

Tax treatment:

(1) Employer-allocated tips (distributed through the employer's payroll): Subject to PAYE income tax AND Class 1 employer and employee NICs. The employer bears the NIC cost.

(2) Tips allocated by an independent tronc operator: Subject to PAYE income tax (the tronc operator must operate a separate PAYE scheme) but NOT subject to employer Class 1 NICs — provided the tronc is genuinely independent. HMRC guidance (CWG2 — Employer further guide to PAYE and NICs) governs tronc arrangements.

(3) Cash tips received directly by workers without employer involvement: The worker is responsible for declaring these to HMRC via self-assessment. No employer PAYE or NIC obligation.
sections.keyPrinciple: Tips are ALWAYS additional to National Minimum Wage — never a substitute. The choice between employer-allocated tips and an independent tronc has significant NIC implications: employer NICs apply to employer-allocated tips but NOT to genuinely independent tronc allocations.
refs: NMW Regulations 2015 Reg.10(j) (tips excluded from NMW calculation)
HMRC CWG2 — Employer further guide to PAYE and NICs (tronc arrangements)
ERA 1996 s.27F (independent troncs — legal requirements)
NMWA 1998 s.1 (entitlement to NMW — tips are additional)
Income Tax (Earnings and Pensions) Act 2003 s.62 (tips as earnings)

regulatoryGuidance.title: Tips, gratuities and service charges (guidance for employers)
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: HMRC guidance on tax treatment of tips including tronc arrangements, PAYE obligations, and NIC treatment

commonErrors: Using tips to 'make up' sub-NMW hourly pay — tips are excluded from NMW calculation by Regulation 10(j) and must be treated as additional to wages
Failing to operate PAYE on employer-allocated tips — tips paid through the employer's payroll system are subject to PAYE and NICs
Setting up a 'tronc' that is not genuinely independent in order to avoid employer NICs — HMRC can challenge the independence of the tronc and assess employer NICs retrospectively
Workers failing to declare cash tips received directly — while this is the worker's responsibility, employers should inform workers of their obligation to declare all income

pendingAmendments.status: The Employment Rights Act 2025 strengthens the enforcement landscape through the Fair Work Agency, which may exercise enforcement functions relevant to tipping practices. The FWA's exact relationship with Tips Act enforcement is subject to commencement and secondary legislation.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: MODERATE — potential for state enforcement of tipping obligations through the FWA in addition to individual tribunal complaints

pendingAmendments.status: The Act came into force 1 October 2024. Given the 12-month limitation period for allocation complaints, the first tribunal decisions are anticipated from late 2025 onwards. Key areas for judicial development: meaning of 'fair allocation', scope of 'qualifying tips' in platform/gig economy contexts, application to multi-site employers.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: HIGH — first decisions will establish interpretive baselines for the entire Act

pendingAmendments.status: Employment law is devolved to the Northern Ireland Assembly. The Tips Act does not extend to Northern Ireland. The NI Assembly may consider equivalent legislation.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: LOW for England/Wales/Scotland employers — relevant for multi-jurisdictional operations
